items.title: Tension pneumothorax
items.contentHtml:
Tension pneumothorax

  • Pathophysiology: unidirectional valve mechanism that traps air in the pleural space, displacing the contralateral mediastinum and compromising venous return.
  • Clinical diagnosis: hypotension, jugular distension, absence of vesicular murmur, tympanism, contralateral tracheal deviation.
  • Management immediate: needle thoracostomy (14-16G angiocatheter in 2nd EIC midclavicular line) followed by chest tube in 5th EIC mid-axillary line.
  • Trap: do not wait for radiological confirmation; Needle drainage is temporary and must be converted to a permanent tube.

items.title: massive hemothorax
items.contentHtml:
Massive hemothorax

  • Definition: accumulation of blood in the pleural cavity >1500 mL or >20 mL/kg in an adult, or initial drainage by tube >20 mL/kg.
  • Surgical criteria: initial drainage >1500 mL or hourly drainage >200 mL/h for 2-4 hours.
  • Management: large caliber chest tube (32-36 Fr), replacement of blood products (1:1:1 ratio) and urgent thoracotomy if criteria are met.
  • Trap: do not remove the tube if the output is high; thoracotomy is not delayed by attempting complete resuscitation.

items.title: cardiac tamponade
items.contentHtml:
Cardiac tamponade

  • Beck's triad: hypotension, muffled heart sounds, jugular venous distention.
  • Kussmaul's sign: paradoxical increase in jugular venous pressure during inspiration.
  • Diagnosis: echocardiography (FAST) shows pericardial effusion with diastolic cavities collapse right.
  • Management: pericardiocentesis (subxiphoid) guided by ultrasound or cecum; surgical drainage (pericardial window) if recurrent or hemopericardium.
  • Trap: Beck's triad is present only in 30% of cases; FAST ultrasound is standard.

items.title: Sternal fracture and myocardial contusion
items.contentHtml:
Sternal fracture and myocardial contusion

  • Sternal fracture: associated with deceleration (airbag, steering wheel), requires ruling out myocardial contusion.
  • Myocardial contusion: diagnosis by ECG (ST elevation, BCRI, arrhythmias) and elevated troponin; echocardiogram evaluates segmental dysfunction.
  • Management: cardiac monitoring for 24-48 hours, analgesia, avoid anticoagulation if there is associated hemorrhage.
  • Trap: isolated sternal fracture does not require surgery; myocardial contusion is not equivalent to AMI (do not use thrombolytics).

items.title: Aortography in aortic trauma
items.contentHtml:
Aortography in blunt aortic trauma

  • Historical gold standard: aortography, today replaced by angio-computed tomography of the chest (sensitivity ~100%) for traumatic aortic injury.
  • Key finding: aortic pseudoaneurysm in isthmus (ligament arteriosus) due to deceleration mechanism.
  • Indication for aortography: when computed tomography angiography is equivocal or for endovascular planning (TEVAR).
  • Trap: chest x-ray may show mediastinal widening >8 cm, but it is not diagnostic; Do not delay computed tomography angiography in unstable patients.

items.title: pericardial window
items.contentHtml:
Pericardial window in cardiac injury

  • Indication: penetrating cardiac injury with recurrent tamponade or hemopericardium confirmed by ultrasound.
  • Technique: subxiphoid approach, resection of pericardium for permanent drainage and exploration of cardiac injury.
  • Alternative: pericardiocentesis only as a temporary measure in extreme instability; the window is definitive.
  • Trap: in non-stabilizable cardiac injury, indicate emergency thoracotomy instead of window; Do not use window if there is cardiac arrest.

items.title: Emergency thoracotomy
items.contentHtml:
Emergency thoracotomy indications

  • Absolute indications: traumatic cardiac arrest with CPR <10 min in penetrating wound, <5 min in blunt wound; massive hemothorax with drainage >1500 mL immediate or >200 mL/h sustained.
  • Other indications: cardiac tamponade without response to pericardiocentesis, tracheobronchial injury with massive leak, air embolism.
  • Technique: anterolateral thoracotomy (4°-5° EIC), transsternal extension if access is required bilateral.
  • Trap: in blunt trauma with arrest, survival is <2%; Do not indicate if there are no vital signs on scene.

items.title: tracheobronchial injury
items.contentHtml:
Tracheobronchial injury: fallen lung sign

  • Faded lung sign: in chest x-ray, the collapsed lung is located in the lower part of the hemithorax, separated from the hilum (due to complete bronchial rupture).
  • Clinic: hemoptysis, subcutaneous emphysema, persistent pneumothorax despite tube pleural.
  • Diagnosis: bronchoscopy (gold standard) to locate the lesion.
  • Management: surgical (primary repair) if lesion >1/3 of circumference or massive air leak; selective intubation in healthy bronchus if necessary.
  • Trap: the dropped lung sign is pathognomonic, but may be absent if the lesion is partial.

items.title: Lung contusion
items.contentHtml:
Pulmonary contusion: conservative management

  • Definition: parenchymal injury due to shock waves, causes hypoxemia due to intrapulmonary shunt.
  • Conservative management: oxygen therapy to maintain SpO2 >90%, analgesia, respiratory physiotherapy, fluid restriction (avoid overload).
  • Indication of VMI: PaO2/FiO2 <200, hypoventilation or failure of non-invasive measures.
  • Trap: lung contusion worsens in the first 24-48h; Do not use corticosteroids or prophylactic antibiotics (they do not reduce mortality).

items.title: Rib fractures and flail chest
items.contentHtml:
Rib fractures and flail chest

  • Flat chest: three or more consecutive rib fractures in at least two sites, generating paradoxical movement.
  • Management: multimodal analgesia (NSAIDs, opioids, paravertebral block), physiotherapy, avoid prophylactic mechanical ventilation.
  • Indication for IMV: respiratory failure (PaO2 <60 mmHg, PaCO2 >50 mmHg), shock or associated severe pulmonary contusion.
  • Trap: Surgical rib fixation is indicated in unstable chest with failure to wean from IMV or severe deformity; It is not routine.

items.title: pleural drainage
items.contentHtml:
Pleural drainage: mid axillary line 5° EIC

  • Insertion site: 5th intercostal space (EIC), mid axillary line (safety zone of the safety triangle).
  • Technique: incision parallel to the rib, blunt dissection, pleural entry with a finger, insertion of the posterior directed tube and apical (pneumothorax) or posterior and basal (hemothorax).
  • Caliber: 32-36 Fr for hemothorax; 20-24 Fr for uncomplicated pneumothorax.
  • Trap: do not place the tube in 2° EIC midclavicular line as a routine (only for needle decompression); The standard point is the 5th EIC mid-axillary line.

items.title: Open pneumothorax
items.contentHtml:
Open pneumothorax: three-point dressing

  • Definition: open thoracic wound >2/3 of the tracheal diameter that allows air to enter, collapsing the lung.
  • Initial management: three-point dressing (occlusive fixed on three sides, leaving one side free to avoid pneumothorax tension).
  • Definitive management: surgical cleaning, closure of the wound and placement of pleural tube away from the wound.
  • Trap: do not use completely occlusive dressing (without valve) because it converts open pneumothorax into tension pneumothorax; The three-point dressing works as a one-way valve.
